title: Calliandra parvifolia
taxonomy: Calliandra parvifolia is classified as a species of flowering plant in the family Fabaceae, commonly known as legumes. It is native to tropical regions and belongs to the genus Calliandra, which comprises about 170 species. The genus Calliandra is further grouped under the subfamily Faboideae. The subfamily Faboideae is a large and diverse group of flowering plants, including peas, beans, and many other legumes. Calliandra parvifolia is a part of this complex and diverse group of plants.
uses: Calliandra parvifolia is a plant with various traditional medicinal uses, particularly in Latin American countries. The plant's extracts have been reported to exhibit antioxidant, anti-inflammatory, and antimicrobial properties, which can be beneficial for treating various health conditions. Traditionally, the plant is used to treat symptoms of fever, respiratory issues, and gastrointestinal problems. The leaves and stems of Calliandra parvifolia are often used to make teas, infusions, and decoctions that are consumed for their medicinal benefits. Further research is needed to confirm the efficacy and safety of using Calliandra parvifolia as a medicinal plant.

intro: Calliandra parvifolia, also known as dwarf powderpuff, is a flowering plant species native to the tropical regions of Central and South America. The plant has been traditionally used for its medicinal properties, particularly in treating various health issues such as fever, inflammation, and respiratory problems, due to its anti-inflammatory, antiseptic, and expectorant properties. The bioactive constituents of Calliandra parvifolia include alkaloids, flavonoids, and phenolic acids, which are responsible for its medicinal properties. The plant is commonly used to make herbal teas, infusions, and decoctions, as well as being incorporated into traditional medicine remedies, such as syrups and tinctures, to leverage its health benefits.
